ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for beef fajitas; A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef fajitas as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: beef skirt steak or flank steak

serving.description: 1 serving (2 fajitas with tortillas, beef, peppers and onions)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of beef fajitas consists of approximately 300 g, comprising two flour tortillas filled with grilled beef strips, sautéed bell peppers, and onions.

nutrient_source.title: Beef fajita mixture (beef, peppers, onions) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Flour tortilla — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; composite estimate for assembled dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for assembled beef fajitas as a restaurant dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for: (1) cooked beef skirt steak (FDC ID 174032), (2) sautéed bell peppers and onions (FDC IDs 170108 and 170000), and (3) flour tortilla (FDC ID 168140), weighted to reflect a typical assembled fajita composition of approximately 40% beef, 25% vegetables, and 35% tortilla per 100 g of assembled dish. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA Branded Food entries for beef fajita kits.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — flour tortilla and mixed beef-vegetable dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for assembled beef fajitas. GI of 50 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using flour tortilla (GI ~52, University of Sydney) as the primary glycemic driver, moderated by the protein and fat content of the beef component and the fibre from vegetables, consistent with mixed meal GI attenuation principles documented in Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 52.5) / 100 = 26.3, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 17.5 × 3 = 52.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit beef fajita portion size to one tortilla and request reduced-sodium seasoning, as the combined sodium from marinades, seasoning blends, and tortillas can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of approximately 26 per standard serving driven by flour tortillas; protein and fat from beef help attenuate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider limiting to one flour tortilla per serving or substituting with a low-GI corn tortilla, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 2.2 g per 100 g and sodium of 420 mg per 100 g; skirt steak is a moderately fatty cut that contributes to saturated fat intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts such as sirloin, limit portion size to one tortilla, and request grilling without added oil to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef fajitas are a popular Tex-Mex dish originating from the ranching culture of the Texas-Mexico border region, traditionally made with grilled beef skirt steak or flank steak sliced into strips and served sizzling on a cast-iron skillet alongside sautéed bell peppers and onions. The dish is typically assembled by the diner at the table, wrapping the hot beef and vegetable mixture into warm flour tortillas and garnishing with condiments such as sour cream, guacamole, salsa, and shredded cheese. The beef is marinated in a blend of lime juice, cumin, chili powder, garlic, and oil before grilling, which imparts a smoky, tangy, and mildly spiced flavour profile. The texture contrast between the tender, charred beef strips, the slightly crisp sautéed peppers and onions, and the soft, pliable tortilla is a defining characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, beef fajitas provide a meaningful source of protein (approximately 13.5 g per 100 g) and iron (approximately 2.1 mg per 100 g), with moderate energy density of approximately 185 kcal per 100 g. The sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 26, driven primarily by the flour tortillas, though the protein and fat from the beef component help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions. In Singapore, beef fajitas are served at Mexican and Tex-Mex restaurants and represent a Western cuisine option within the local dining landscape.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for assembled beef fajitas as a restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (cooked beef skirt steak, sautéed bell peppers and onions, flour tortilla) weighted to a typical assembled composition. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using flour tortilla as the primary glycemic driver. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source data for the component ingredients at the level of precision required for this composite estimate. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the assembled dish (approximately 40% cooked beef skirt steak, 25% sautéed bell peppers and onions, 35% flour tortilla by weight per 100 g of assembled dish). Sodium value reflects typical restaurant-level seasoning and marinade contribution; home-prepared versions may have substantially lower sodium. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on flour tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by the protein and fat content of the beef component. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (50 × 52.5) / 100 = 26.3 g. The halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed for the specific restaurants listed in the source data without independent certification verification. This record was seeded from field survey data data (GD000450) and is not present in the FD series food index. The cuisine is Mexican/Tex-Mex; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply as this is not a Singaporean dish. Chinese names are provided as supplementary local-language support given the Singapore context.
